name: Somnath Temple
significance: Revered as the first among the twelve Jyotirlingas of Shiva.
history: Somnath has been destroyed and rebuilt many times across its long history. The present temple was reconstructed in 1951 in the Maru-Gurjara (Chalukya) style, an effort spearheaded by Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, and was dedicated by then-President Rajendra Prasad.
architecture: Maru-Gurjara (Chalukya) style in sandstone, with an intricately carved shikhara rising above the sanctum on the Arabian Sea shore.
legend: Tradition holds that the moon god Soma built the original shrine in gold to be cured of a curse, giving the site its name 'Somnath' (Lord of the Moon).
timings: Generally open early morning to night, with aarti three times daily. Confirm exact timings with the temple before visiting.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Veraval (~7 km). Nearest airports: Diu and Rajkot.

name: Mallikarjuna Swamy Temple
significance: A rare site that is both a Jyotirlinga (Mallikarjuna) and a Shakti Peetha (Bhramaramba Devi).
history: Set in the Nallamala hills on the banks of the Krishna river, the temple complex carries inscriptions and additions from successive dynasties including the Reddy and Vijayanagara rulers.
architecture: A large fortified complex with carved stone gopurams and mandapas in the Dravidian tradition.
legend: Associated with the legend of Shiva and Parvati following their son Kartikeya to the Srisailam hill.
timings: Generally open from early morning to night with multiple sevas. Confirm exact timings with the temple before visiting.
howToReach: Nearest railway stations: Markapur and Nandyal. Nearest airport: Hyderabad.

name: Mahakaleshwar Temple
significance: Famous for its swayambhu (self-manifested) lingam and the pre-dawn Bhasma Aarti.
history: Located beside the Rudra Sagar lake in the ancient city of Ujjain, the temple is mentioned in numerous Puranic and classical texts; the present structure dates largely to the Maratha period.
architecture: Bhumija and Maratha architectural influences, with the sanctum set below ground level.
legend: The lingam is revered as Dakshinamurti, uniquely facing south, a feature considered especially auspicious here.
timings: Opens very early for the Bhasma Aarti (pre-dawn) and remains open through the day. Bhasma Aarti requires advance booking; confirm with the temple.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Ujjain Junction. Nearest airport: Indore (~55 km).

name: Omkareshwar Temple
significance: Set on the sacred island of Mandhata in the Narmada river, said to be shaped like the symbol 'Om'.
history: A historic pilgrimage centre on the Narmada, the site has temples and ghats developed over many centuries.
architecture: North Indian Nagara style stone temple with a tall shikhara, reached across the Narmada.
legend: Tradition links the island's Om-like shape to the sanctity of the site.
timings: Generally open from early morning to night with regular aartis. Confirm exact timings with the temple before visiting.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Omkareshwar Road / Indore. Nearest airport: Indore (~77 km).

name: Kedarnath Temple
significance: The highest of the twelve Jyotirlingas (~3,583 m) and part of both the Char Dham and Panch Kedar circuits.
history: An ancient stone temple in the Garhwal Himalayas near the Mandakini river, traditionally associated with the Pandavas and revived by Adi Shankaracharya.
architecture: Massive grey-stone masonry built to withstand Himalayan conditions, without mortar in its core structure.
legend: Linked to the legend of the Pandavas seeking Shiva, who took the form of a bull and dived into the ground, leaving his hump at Kedarnath.
festivals: Opening (Akshaya Tritiya) and closing (Bhai Dooj) ceremonies
timings: Open only for roughly six months a year (around late April/May to November); closed in winter. Confirm the yatra dates each season.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Rishikesh. Nearest airport: Dehradun. The final ~16-18 km from Gaurikund is on foot, by pony, palki, or helicopter.

name: Bhimashankar Temple
significance: A Jyotirlinga in the Sahyadri (Western Ghats) and the source of the Bhima river.
history: The temple combines older shrine elements with Nagara additions from the 18th century; the surrounding hills are now a wildlife sanctuary.
architecture: Nagara-style stone temple set amid dense Western Ghats forest.
legend: Associated with Shiva's defeat of the demon Tripurasura, after which he is said to have rested here.
timings: Generally open from early morning to night with regular aartis. Confirm exact timings with the temple before visiting.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Pune. Nearest airport: Pune (~110 km).

name: Kashi Vishwanath Temple
significance: One of the most revered Shiva shrines, on the west bank of the Ganga in the ancient city of Kashi.
history: The present temple was built in 1780 by Maharani Ahilyabai Holkar of Indore; its spire was later gold-plated with a donation from Maharaja Ranjit Singh. The Kashi Vishwanath Dham corridor connecting the temple to the Ganga ghats was inaugurated in 2021.
architecture: Compact Nagara-style temple crowned by a gold-plated shikhara and dome.
legend: Kashi is traditionally held to be the eternal city of Shiva, who is said to grant liberation (moksha) to those who pass away here.
timings: Generally open from pre-dawn mangala aarti until late night with several aartis. Confirm exact timings and darshan rules with the temple.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Varanasi Junction. Nearest airport: Lal Bahadur Shastri International Airport, Varanasi.

name: Trimbakeshwar Temple
significance: A Jyotirlinga at the source of the Godavari river, at the foot of the Brahmagiri hill.
history: The present temple was built in the 18th century by Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao (Nana Saheb) in black basalt.
architecture: Ornate Nagara-style temple in black stone with finely carved mandapas.
legend: The lingam here is distinctive for its three faces representing Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva.
timings: Generally open from early morning to night with regular aartis. Confirm exact timings with the temple before visiting.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Nashik Road. Nearest airport: Nashik / Mumbai.

name: Baidyanath Temple (Vaidyanath Dham)
significance: A major Jyotirlinga, widely identified with Deoghar; the site is also revered as a Shakti Peetha.
history: Deoghar ('abode of the gods') has long been a pilgrimage centre; the temple complex includes numerous smaller shrines around the main sanctum.
architecture: A cluster of stone temples with the main shrine topped by a tall shikhara.
legend: Linked to the legend of Ravana offering his heads to Shiva; the lingam is also called Kamna Linga, said to fulfil devotees' wishes.
timings: Generally open from pre-dawn to night; the Shravani Mela period sees very heavy footfall. Confirm timings before visiting.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Jasidih (~7 km). Nearest airport: Deoghar / Patna / Ranchi.

name: Nageshwar Temple
significance: A Jyotirlinga near Dwarka, traditionally identified with the ancient forest of Daarukavanam.
history: A long-revered Shiva site near the Saurashtra coast; the complex is marked by a very large seated statue of Shiva visible from afar.
architecture: A modern temple structure over the ancient lingam, with a tall Shiva murti in the precinct.
legend: Associated with the legend of Shiva protecting the devotee Supriya from the demon Daaruka.
timings: Generally open from early morning to night with regular aartis. Confirm exact timings with the temple before visiting.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Dwarka. Nearest airport: Jamnagar.

name: Ramanathaswamy Temple
significance: A Jyotirlinga on Rameswaram island, famed for having one of the longest temple corridors in India.
history: Developed over centuries with major contributions from the Pandya, Nayak and later rulers; closely tied to the Ramayana tradition.
architecture: Grand Dravidian temple with towering gopurams and immense pillared corridors.
legend: Tradition holds that Rama worshipped Shiva here to atone after the war in Lanka; the temple has 22 sacred theerthams (water bodies).
timings: Generally open from early morning to night with ritual baths at the theerthams. Confirm exact timings with the temple before visiting.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Rameswaram. Nearest airport: Madurai (~170 km).

name: Grishneshwar Temple
significance: Traditionally regarded as the last (twelfth) of the Jyotirlingas, close to the Ellora caves.
history: The present temple was rebuilt in the 18th century by Maharani Ahilyabai Holkar in red volcanic rock.
architecture: South-Indian-influenced stone temple with finely carved pillars and a layered shikhara.
legend: Named after the devotee Kusuma (Ghushma), whose unwavering devotion is said to have brought Shiva to manifest here.
timings: Generally open from early morning to night with regular aartis. Confirm exact timings with the temple before visiting.
howToReach: Nearest railway station: Aurangabad (Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar). Nearest airport: Aurangabad (~30 km).
